items.title: Peripheral Arterial Disease: Intermittent Claudication
items.contentHtml:
Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD): Intermittent Claudication


Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) manifests itself with intermittent claudication, muscle pain induced by exercise and relieved by rest. The most commonly affected artery is the superficial femoral artery. The diagnosis is suspected clinically and confirmed with the ankle-brachial index (ABI). Initial treatment is conservative: control of cardiovascular risk factors (smoking cessation, control of diabetes mellitus, arterial hypertension and dyslipidemia), supervised exercise program and drugs such as cilostazol. Revascularization is reserved for severe claudication or critical ischemia.

items.foco: Ankle-brachial index: diagnosis of chronic ischemia
items.title: Ankle-Arm Index: Chronic Ischemia
items.contentHtml:
Ankle-brachial index (ABI): Diagnosis


The ankle-brachial index (ABI) is the diagnostic test of choice for peripheral arterial disease. It is calculated by dividing the systolic blood pressure of the ankle (highest of the posterior tibial or pedis) by the highest systolic blood pressure of both arms. An ABI <0.90 is diagnostic of peripheral arterial disease. Values ​​of 0.91 to 0.99 are considered borderline, and >1.30 suggests medial arterial calcification, common in diabetics or those with chronic renal failure, and may give false negatives.

items.foco: Deep vein thrombosis: risk factors (Virchow triad)
items.title: Deep Vein Thrombosis: Risk Factors
items.contentHtml:
Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT): Virchow's Triad


Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is the formation of a clot in a deep vein, typically in the lower extremities. Its risk factors are summarized in the Virchow triad:
  • Venous stasis: prolonged immobilization, major surgery, heart failure.
  • Endothelial injury: trauma, surgery, central venous catheters.
  • Hypercoagulability: cancer, hereditary thrombophilias (factor V Leiden, antithrombin deficiency, protein C or S), pregnancy, oral contraceptives.
The main risk of DVT is pulmonary embolism.

items.foco: Acute arterial embolism: the six Ps of ischemia
items.title: Acute Arterial Embolism: Six Ps of Ischemia
items.contentHtml:
Acute Arterial Embolism: The Six Ps


Acute arterial embolism is a vascular emergency, with sudden onset of severe ischemia. The most common source of embolism is atrial fibrillation or post-acute myocardial infarction mural thrombus. The classic symptoms are the six Ps: Pain, Pulselessness, Pallor, Paresthesias, Paralysis and Poikilothermia. The diagnosis is clinical and confirmed with Doppler ultrasound or angiotomography. Treatment is urgent revascularization, typically embolectomy or local thrombolysis.

items.foco: Chronic venous insufficiency: internal malleolar ulcers
items.title: Chronic Venous Insufficiency: Ulcers
items.contentHtml:
Chronic Venous Insufficiency (CVI): Ulcers


Chronic venous insufficiency (CVI) is caused by valvular dysfunction or venous obstruction, leading to venous hypertension. Venous ulcers are the most serious manifestation, typically located in the internal malleolar region (supramalleolar). They are painless or slightly painful, superficial, with irregular edges, exudative and surrounded by hyperpigmentation (ocher dermatitis) and lipodermatosclerosis. Treatment includes elastic compression, leg elevation, wound care, and sometimes venous ablation.

items.foco: Abdominal aortic aneurysm: screening in older men
items.title: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm: Screening
items.contentHtml:
Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA): Screening


An abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is a localized dilation of the aorta of at least 1.5 times the normal diameter or an absolute diameter >3 cm. The main risk factor is smoking. Screening with abdominal ultrasound is recommended only once in men aged 65 to 75 years with a history of smoking. The threshold for elective repair is a diameter >5.5 cm in males or rapid growth (>0.5 cm in 6 months).

items.title: Thromboangiitis Obliterans (Buerger)
items.contentHtml:
Thromboangiitis Obliterans (Buerger's Disease)


Thromboangiitis obliterans (TAO), or Buerger's disease, is a segmental and occlusive inflammatory vasculitis of small and medium-sized arteries and veins in distal extremities. It almost exclusively affects young smokers (men, <45 years old). It is characterized by distal ischemia, which can progress to trophic ulcers and amputations. The most important and only treatment that stops progression is complete and immediate cessation of smoking. There is no specific pharmacological treatment.

items.title: Raynaud's Phenomenon: Primary vs. Secondary
items.contentHtml:
Raynaud's Phenomenon: Differentiation


Raynaud's Phenomenon is a paroxysmal vasospasm of the digital arterioles in response to cold or emotional stress, causing triphasic color changes (pallor, cyanosis, erythema). It differs in:
  • Primary: The most common, benign, without underlying cause. It begins in young people, is bilateral and symmetrical, without ulcers.
  • Secondary: Associated with connective tissue diseases (systemic scleroderma, systemic lupus erythematosus), drugs, arterial occlusions. It is usually unilateral, asymmetric, and can cause digital ulcer or gangrene. It is investigated with antinuclear antibodies and capillaroscopy.

items.foco: DVT treatment: anticoagulation initiation and duration
items.title: DVT Treatment: Anticoagulation
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT)


The treatment of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is based on anticoagulation. Initially, direct-acting oral anticoagulants (DOACs) such as rivaroxaban or apixaban, or low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) followed by DOACs or vitamin K antagonists are used. The standard duration is 3 months for DVT caused by a transient risk factor. For unprovoked DVT or DVT associated with cancer, the duration is >3 months, and may be indefinite in cases of high risk of recurrence.

items.foco: Most serious complication of AAA: rupture and hemorrhage
items.title: AAA Complication: Rupture
items.contentHtml:
Most Serious Complication of Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA)


The most serious complication of abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is rupture, a surgical emergency with a very high mortality (>80%). The classic presentation includes sudden, intense abdominal or lower back pain, a palpable pulsating mass, and hypovolemic shock. Other symptoms include syncope or unexplained hypotension. Diagnostic confirmation in stable patients is with computed tomography angiography; In unstable patients, immediate transfer to the operating room for open or endovascular repair (EVAR) is a priority.

items.foco: Primary lymphedema: genetic or idiopathic cause
items.title: Primary Lymphedema
items.contentHtml:
Primary Lymphedema: Etiology and Types


Primary lymphedema is an alteration in the development or function of the lymphatic system, resulting in an accumulation of lymph and edema. Its cause is genetic or idiopathic. It is classified according to the age of onset:
  • Congenital: present at birth or before one year of age (e.g. Milroy's Disease).
  • Precocious (praecox): appears before the age of 35, more common in women and usually debuts during puberty or pregnancy.
  • Late (late): after the age of 35 years.
Clinically, it is a soft edema that does not initially leave a pit, hardens over time and typically affects the dorsum of the foot and fingers (positive Stemmer sign).

items.foco: Chronic critical ischemia: pain at rest and trophic ulcers
items.title: Chronic Critical Ischemia
items.contentHtml:
Chronic Critical Ischemia (CHF): Rest Pain and Ulcers


Chronic critical ischemia (CHF) represents the most advanced and severe form of peripheral arterial disease. It is defined by the presence of ischemic pain at rest, which worsens with elevation of the limb and improves when it is hung, or the presence of non-healing trophic ulcers or gangrene, lasting >2 weeks. The diagnosis is confirmed with an ankle-brachial index (ABI) <0.40 or ankle systolic blood pressure <50 mmHg. Revascularization is the mainstay of limb-preserving treatment.
